rust-introduction.title: Explain Rust From Program Output
rust-introduction.description: Write a Rust program that prints three short lines: what Rust is designed for, the command used to create a new project, and the file where main normally lives in a Cargo binary project.
rust-introduction.sampleOutput:
Rust focuses on safety and performance
cargo new creates a project
src/main.rs contains main

rust-introduction.title: Hello Candidate
rust-introduction.description: Read a candidate name from standard input and print a welcome line in the format used by many interview warm-ups. Trim surrounding whitespace before printing.

rust-introduction.title: Cargo Command Classifier
rust-introduction.description: Read one Cargo command: build, run, test, or check. Print the main purpose of that command. Print unknown for any other command.
rust-introduction.sampleOutput: check code without producing a final executable

rust-introduction.title: Main Function Shape
rust-introduction.description: Print a minimal Rust main function exactly as three lines. This checks that you know the entry point shape before deeper syntax questions.

rust-introduction.title: Compiler Message Summary
rust-introduction.description: Read a single compiler status word: success, warning, or error. Print what a developer should do next in an interview setting.
rust-introduction.sampleOutput: read the warning and decide whether the code should be improved

rust-introduction.title: Project File Router
rust-introduction.description: Read a filename and print whether it belongs to package metadata, binary source, library source, or another file. Handle Cargo.toml, src/main.rs, and src/lib.rs.

rust-introduction.title: Rust Promise Checker
rust-introduction.description: Read three words and count how many are core Rust goals among safety, speed, concurrency, and productivity. Print the count.

rust-introduction.title: Toolchain Interview Answer
rust-introduction.description: Read a tool name: rustc, cargo, rustfmt, or clippy. Print a concise interview-ready explanation of what that tool does.
rust-introduction.sampleOutput: clippy suggests idiomatic improvements and catches common mistakes

rust-introduction.title: Build Step Planner
rust-introduction.description: Read a space-separated list of requested actions. Normalize aliases: compile means build, execute means run, verify means test. Print the unique Cargo commands in first-seen order.

rust-introduction.title: Interview Checklist Formatter
rust-introduction.description: Read a comma-separated list of Rust setup items. Trim each item, ignore empty entries, and print each valid item prefixed with a one-based checklist number.

rust-introduction.title: Error-First Explanation
rust-introduction.description: Read a short compiler error category: syntax, type, borrow, or lifetime. Print one practical debugging step for that category. Unknown categories should print read the full diagnostic.
rust-introduction.sampleOutput: compare the expected type with the value being provided

rust-introduction.title: Cargo Output Parser
rust-introduction.description: Read lines of simplified Cargo output and count how many lines start with Compiling, Finished, and Running. Print the three counts in that order.
rust-introduction.sampleInput:
Compiling app
Compiling dep
Finished dev
Running target/debug/app

variables.title: Immutable Binding Trace
variables.description: Read an integer into an immutable binding named score and print it. The solution should not mark the binding as mutable.

variables.title: Make It Mutable
variables.description: Read two integers. Store the first in a mutable binding, add the second to it, and print the final value.

variables.title: Shadow A String Length
variables.description: Read one word into a String binding, then shadow the binding with its length. Print the length. Do not use a second variable name for the length.

variables.title: Constant Tax Rate
variables.description: Define a constant TAX_PERCENT with value 18. Read a base amount and print the tax amount using integer arithmetic.

variables.title: Shadow To Change Type
variables.description: Read a number as text, trim it, parse it as i32 by shadowing the original binding, double it, and print the result.

variables.title: Mutability Audit
variables.description: Read variable declarations separated by commas. Count how many contain let mut and how many contain const. Print both counts.
variables.sampleInput: let x, let mut count, const LIMIT, let mut total

variables.title: Swap Without Mutating Inputs
variables.description: Read two words into immutable bindings. Create a new tuple with the values reversed and print the reversed order.

variables.title: Scope Boundary Reporter
variables.description: Read an outer value and an inner value. Use an inner block that shadows the outer value, print the inner value inside the block, then print the original outer value after the block.

variables.title: Shadowing Pipeline
variables.description: Read a sentence. Shadow the binding three times: trimmed text, lowercase text, and word count. Print the final count.

variables.title: Immutable By Default Fix
variables.description: Read operations like start=10 add=5 add=7. Keep the original start value immutable, use a separate mutable running total, and print both the original and total.

variables.title: Type Annotation Gate
variables.description: Read tokens in the form name:type. Count how many variables have explicit integer types i32, u32, or usize. Print the count.

variables.title: Binding Lifetime Story
variables.description: Read pairs in the form scope:name. Count how many names appear in both outer and inner scopes, showing that shadowing can reuse a name without mutating the original binding.

functions.title: Write An Add Function
functions.description: Read two i32 values. Implement add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 and print the returned sum from main.

functions.title: Expression Return
functions.description: Read an integer. Implement square(n: i32) -> i32 using an expression return without a semicolon on the final expression.

functions.title: Boolean Helper
functions.description: Read an integer and call is_even(n: i32) -> bool. Print even when true and odd when false.

functions.title: Unit Return Logger
functions.description: Write a function print_status(status: &str) that returns unit and prints status=<value>. Call it from main using the input value.

functions.title: Parse Then Compute
functions.description: Implement parse_i32(text: &str) -> Option<i32> and triple(value: i32) -> i32. Read one token and print the tripled value or invalid.

functions.title: Borrowed String Formatter
functions.description: Implement label(name: &str, score: i32) -> String. Read a name and score, call the function without moving unnecessary data, and print the label.

functions.title: Tuple Return
functions.description: Implement min_max(a: i32, b: i32) -> (i32, i32). Read two integers and print min=<value> max=<value>.

functions.title: Early Return Guard
functions.description: Implement divide(a: i32, b: i32) -> Option<i32>. Return None when b is zero. Read two integers and print the quotient or division by zero.

functions.title: Function Composition Pipeline
functions.description: Implement trim_input, normalize_case, and count_words as separate functions. Read a sentence, pass it through the pipeline, and print the word count.

functions.title: Return Result From Validator
functions.description: Implement validate_port(text: &str) -> Result<u16, String>. Accept numbers from 1 through 65535. Print ok:<port> or err:<message>.

functions.title: Higher Order Calculator
functions.description: Implement apply(a: i32, b: i32, op: fn(i32, i32) -> i32) -> i32 with add and multiply functions. Read an operator word add or multiply and two numbers, then print the result.

functions.title: Small API Design
functions.description: Design three functions for a tiny cart: subtotal(prices: &[i32]) -> i32, discount(total: i32) -> i32, and payable(total: i32) -> i32. Apply a 10 percent discount when subtotal is at least 1000 and print the payable amount.

rust-introduction.question: In an interview, what is the clearest description of Rust's main value proposition?
options: Memory safety without a garbage collector, plus strong performance
Dynamic typing with runtime ownership checks
A scripting language mainly for browser automation
A language that prevents all possible logic bugs
rust-introduction.answer: Memory safety without a garbage collector, plus strong performance

rust-introduction.question: Which command creates a new Rust package with Cargo?
options: cargo new app_name
rustc new app_name
cargo make app_name
rustup project app_name

rust-introduction.question: Where is the entry point normally located in a new Cargo binary project?
options: src/main.rs
main.toml
Cargo.lock
src/start.rs

rust-introduction.question: What does println! indicate in Rust?
options: It is a macro call
It is a struct constructor
It is an async function
It disables formatting checks

rust-introduction.question: What is the best interview answer for the difference between rustc and Cargo?
options: rustc compiles Rust code, while Cargo manages packages, builds, tests, and project workflows
Cargo is the compiler and rustc only formats code
rustc downloads crates and Cargo checks ownership
They are two names for exactly the same binary
rust-introduction.answer: rustc compiles Rust code, while Cargo manages packages, builds, tests, and project workflows

rust-introduction.question: Why does Rust catch many memory errors at compile time?
options: The ownership and borrowing rules are checked before the program runs
All values are copied into global memory
The runtime garbage collector rejects invalid pointers
Cargo deletes unsafe functions automatically
rust-introduction.answer: The ownership and borrowing rules are checked before the program runs

rust-introduction.question: What does cargo check do?
options: It type-checks and borrow-checks the project without producing the final executable
It runs the binary in release mode
It installs the Rust toolchain
It publishes the crate to crates.io
rust-introduction.answer: It type-checks and borrow-checks the project without producing the final executable

rust-introduction.question: What should you do first when a Rust compiler diagnostic is long?
options: Read the primary error and the span it points to before changing code
Delete Cargo.lock immediately
Add unsafe to the whole file
Ignore it if the program looks correct
rust-introduction.answer: Read the primary error and the span it points to before changing code

rust-introduction.question: Which statement best explains zero-cost abstractions in Rust?
options: High-level constructs are designed to compile to code without unnecessary runtime overhead
Every abstraction is free to allocate at runtime
Rust removes all CPU costs from function calls
Cargo optimizes only code that uses macros
rust-introduction.answer: High-level constructs are designed to compile to code without unnecessary runtime overhead

rust-introduction.question: Why is Rust often chosen for systems programming interviews?
options: It exposes low-level control while enforcing strong safety rules
It hides all memory layout and pointer concepts
It has no type system questions
It only runs inside managed runtimes
rust-introduction.answer: It exposes low-level control while enforcing strong safety rules

rust-introduction.question: What is a precise reason Cargo.lock is committed for applications but often not for libraries?
options: Applications usually want reproducible dependency versions, while libraries should allow compatible versions for downstream users
Libraries cannot compile when Cargo.lock exists
Applications do not use semantic versioning
Cargo.lock contains source code for all crates
rust-introduction.answer: Applications usually want reproducible dependency versions, while libraries should allow compatible versions for downstream users

rust-introduction.question: If an interviewer asks why Rust has a learning curve, which answer is strongest?
options: Rust asks developers to model ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, and types explicitly so errors are caught earlier
Rust syntax changes every time the compiler runs
Rust has no standard library
Rust requires manual memory management for every allocation
rust-introduction.answer: Rust asks developers to model ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, and types explicitly so errors are caught earlier

variables.question: What is the default mutability of a Rust binding created with let?
options: Immutable
Mutable
Global
Thread-local

variables.question: Which declaration allows reassignment to x?
options: let mut x = 5;
let x mut = 5;
mut let x = 5;
const mut x = 5;

variables.question: What is shadowing in Rust?
options: Declaring a new binding with the same name as an earlier binding
Changing an immutable binding without let
Making a variable visible in every module
Deleting a value before it is used
variables.answer: Declaring a new binding with the same name as an earlier binding

variables.question: Which keyword defines a compile-time constant?
options: const
let
static mut only
final

variables.question: Why can shadowing change a value's type while mutation cannot?
options: Shadowing creates a new binding, while mutation updates the existing binding whose type is fixed
Mutation always creates a new variable
Shadowing disables the type checker
Only constants can change type
variables.answer: Shadowing creates a new binding, while mutation updates the existing binding whose type is fixed

variables.question: What happens after let x = 5; let x = x + 1;?
options: The second x shadows the first and has value 6
The code fails because x is immutable
The first x is mutated in place
The type of x becomes Option<i32>
variables.answer: The second x shadows the first and has value 6

variables.question: Which statement about constants is true?
options: Constants require a type annotation
Constants can be assigned only inside main
Constants are mutable when declared uppercase
Constants can store values computed from runtime input

variables.question: Why does Rust encourage immutability by default?
options: It makes data flow easier to reason about and prevents accidental changes
It makes every program single-threaded
It removes the need for ownership
It forces all values onto the heap
variables.answer: It makes data flow easier to reason about and prevents accidental changes

variables.question: What is the output of this idea: let spaces = "   "; let spaces = spaces.len(); print spaces?
options: Compilation fails because spaces was immutable
A string containing three spaces

variables.question: Which interview answer best distinguishes const from let?
options: const names a value known at compile time and requires a type; let creates a runtime binding and can infer its type
const is always heap allocated and let is always stack allocated
let is global and const is local
const values can be reassigned with mut
variables.answer: const names a value known at compile time and requires a type; let creates a runtime binding and can infer its type

variables.question: Inside an inner block, let x = "inner"; shadows an outer x. What happens when the block ends?
options: The inner binding goes out of scope and the outer binding is visible again
The outer binding is permanently overwritten
Both bindings become mutable
Rust keeps both visible and requires x[0]
variables.answer: The inner binding goes out of scope and the outer binding is visible again

variables.question: Which code style is most idiomatic when a value only changes during construction?
options: Use shadowing or a final immutable binding after construction
Keep it mutable for the whole function
Use a global mutable static
Store every intermediate value as a String
variables.answer: Use shadowing or a final immutable binding after construction

functions.question: Which syntax declares a function that takes two i32 values and returns i32?
options: fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32
function add(a: i32, b: i32): i32
fn add(i32 a, i32 b) => i32
let add(a, b) -> i32
functions.answer: fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32

functions.question: What does a semicolon do to the final expression of a Rust function body?
options: It turns the expression into a statement, so it no longer returns that value
It makes the function async
It converts the value to String
It makes the value mutable
functions.answer: It turns the expression into a statement, so it no longer returns that value

functions.question: What is the return type of a function that does not explicitly return a value?
options: None
void
Never

functions.question: Where can Rust functions be defined?
options: Before or after the function that calls them
Only above main
Only inside structs
Only in Cargo.toml
functions.answer: Before or after the function that calls them

functions.question: Why are parameter types required in Rust function signatures?
options: They make function boundaries explicit and support static type checking
Rust cannot infer any local variable types
They are needed only for documentation
They tell Cargo which crate to download
functions.answer: They make function boundaries explicit and support static type checking

functions.question: Which function body correctly returns x + 1 as an i32?
options: { return; x + 1 }
{ let x + 1 }

functions.question: When should a function take &str instead of String in many interview examples?
options: When it only needs to read text and should not take ownership
When it needs to mutate the caller's string
When it must store text forever
When it needs to avoid UTF-8
functions.answer: When it only needs to read text and should not take ownership

functions.question: Which return type is best for a function that may fail to parse an integer without explaining why?
options: Option<i32>
String

functions.question: Which answer best explains early return in Rust?
options: return can exit a function before the final expression, but idiomatic code often uses expressions when simple
return is required for every successful function result
return can only be used in main
return disables borrow checking for the function
functions.answer: return can exit a function before the final expression, but idiomatic code often uses expressions when simple

functions.question: What does fn(i32, i32) -> i32 represent as a parameter type?
options: A function pointer accepting two i32 values and returning i32
A closure that captures any local variable
A trait object for all functions
A tuple containing two integers and one result
functions.answer: A function pointer accepting two i32 values and returning i32

functions.question: Why should a reusable helper avoid printing when its job is to compute a value?
options: Returning the value keeps the function easier to test and compose
Rust functions are not allowed to print
Printing makes the value mutable
Only main can return values
functions.answer: Returning the value keeps the function easier to test and compose

functions.question: Which function signature is most appropriate for validating input with an error message?
options: fn validate(text: &str) -> Result<u16, String>
fn validate(text: String) -> bool
fn validate() -> ()
fn validate(text: &str) -> u16
